title: Psychiatric Medication Emergencies
subtitle: The nurse recognizes psych-med emergencies — neuroleptic malignant syndrome, serotonin syndrome, lithium toxicity, and MAOI hypertensive crisis. Stop the drug and get help.

patient.name: Psych Med Emergencies
patient.admitDate: The nurse recognizes psychiatric medication emergencies.

labs.v: High fever, lead-pipe rigidity, altered LOC.

nursesNote: Several psychiatric medications can cause emergencies the nurse must recognize fast. Neuroleptic malignant syndrome (NMS) comes from antipsychotics: high fever, severe 'lead-pipe' rigidity, altered consciousness, and unstable vital signs, developing over days. Serotonin syndrome comes from serotonergic drugs (SSRIs, especially combined): agitation, high fever, tremor, hyperreflexia or clonus, and sweating, with a rapid onset. Lithium toxicity (narrow therapeutic range) causes nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, a coarse tremor, ataxia, and confusion, often triggered by low sodium or dehydration. MAOI hypertensive crisis follows eating tyramine-rich foods: severe high blood pressure and a pounding headache. Anticholinergic toxicity causes hot, dry, red skin, confusion, and urinary retention. In any of these the nurse stops the offending drug, calls for help, supports the airway and vital signs, provides cooling and fluids as needed, and monitors closely; prevention includes monitoring levels and teaching diet and early warning signs.
providerOrders: Recognize NMS, serotonin syndrome, lithium toxicity, and MAOI crisis
Call for help and support airway and vital signs
Provide cooling and fluids as needed
Prevent by monitoring levels and teaching diet/early signs

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.sub: What is true about psych-med emergencies?

opts.text: Serotonin syndrome comes from serotonergic drugs
Lithium toxicity causes tremor and confusion
The first action is to stop the drug

steps.title: Analyze Cues
steps.sub: What helps tell them apart / guides care?

opts.text: Serotonin syndrome has a rapid onset
Low sodium/dehydration can trigger lithium toxicity
Anticholinergic toxicity is hot, dry, and red
Supportive care includes cooling and fluids

steps.title: Prioritize Hypotheses
steps.sub: Order responding to a suspected psych-med emergency

opts.text: Recognize the emergency and check the airway
Antipsychotic: fever, lead-pipe rigidity, altered LOC
SSRIs combined: agitation, clonus, sweating, rapid onset
Severe high BP and pounding headache after aged cheese
Low sodium then tremor and confusion on a mood stabilizer
Hyperreflexia and fever soon after adding a serotonergic drug

steps.title: Take Action
steps.sub: A patient on an antipsychotic has fever 104, rigidity, and confusion

opts.text: Stopping the drug at the first signs
Holding lithium and checking a level for toxicity
Letting an MAOI patient eat aged cheese
Treating these as routine side effects
